Knowledge Base

Episodic Memory

Long-term storage for experiences, conversations, and events. Organized chronologically with rich metadata and hybrid retrieval.

Dual-Key Schema

Episodic Memory uses a dual-key design for flexible access patterns:

ColumnTypeDescription
idTEXTUUIDv4 primary key
contentTEXTFull experience record
embeddingBLOBVector embedding
summaryTEXTAAAK-compressed summary
tagsTEXTJSON array
importanceREAL0.0–1.0
sourceTEXTOrigin
session_idTEXTGrouping key
created_atINTEGERUnix timestamp
consolidated_atINTEGERWhen promoted from Working

Hybrid Scoring

Retrieval combines three signals:

score = (vector_similarity * 0.5) + 
      (fts_rank * 0.3) + 
      (temporal_proximity * 0.2)
SignalWeightDescription
Vector similarity0.5Semantic closeness to query
FTS5 rank0.3Full-text match quality
Temporal proximity0.2Recency of the memory

Weights are configurable per-query:

results = mem.recall(
  "project deadline",
  vector_weight=0.7,
  text_weight=0.2,
  temporal_weight=0.1,
)

Consolidation Pipeline


flowchart TD
  WM[Working Memory] -->|candidate| S{Scoring}
  S -->|importance &gt; 0.7| C[Compress with AAAK]
  S -->|importance &lt; 0.3| E[Evict]
  C -->|store| EM[Episodic Memory]
  C -->|extract| SM[Semantic Memory]

Session Grouping

Episodic entries can be grouped by session for chronological recall:

# Store with session
mem.remember(
  content="User approved the design mockup.",
  session_id="design-review-2026-04-25",
  tags=["design", "approval"],
)

# Recall entire session
session = mem.recall_session("design-review-2026-04-25")

Performance

MetricValue
Median retrieval85ms
Storage per entry~2KB (compressed)
Queryable fieldscontent, tags, source, session_id, date range
Best Practice

Tag episodic memories with descriptive labels. Tags are indexed via FTS5 and heavily weighted in retrieval scoring. Good tags: ["api-design", "decision", "user-feedback"]. Bad tags: ["misc", "stuff"].
